key_insight: A two-step seismic workflow combining matched field processing and source relocation can map surface crevasse evolution at metre-scale resolution and quantify crack propagation on a glacier.
engineering_relevance: The method provides a practical way to observe brittle-to-viscous fracture processes in ice, improving understanding of glacier damage, meltwater routing, and deformation rates.
method: Use matched field processing to detect surface icequakes and distinguish them from meltwater noise.
Relocate discrete arrivals to sharpen source positions under sparse or irregular sensor coverage.
Estimate crevasse opening rate and infer diffusion coefficients from the relocated events.
evidence_signal: Applied to Hansbreen, Svalbard, the approach resolved ongoing crevasse opening and yielded diffusion coefficients of 0.47 to 0.55 m2/s from observed icequake locations.
watch_item: Source localization and inferred propagation rates may remain sensitive to limited instrumentation and array geometry, especially in sparse glacial deployments.
paper_title: Surface Crevasse Evolution Observed Using Matched Field Processing and Source Relocation at Hansbreen, Svalbard
